probe_designs.probe_name: Subgroup AUROC and Equalized Odds Across Ethnicity
probe_designs.content: Compute AUROC, AUPRC, sensitivity, specificity, FPR and FNR separately for each of the 6 ethnicity groups. Quantify disparities using Equalized Odds gap (max |TPR_i - TPR_j|, max |FPR_i - FPR_j|) and Equal Opportunity gap. Healthy: gaps below ~0.05 and overlapping confidence intervals across groups. Unhealthy: substantial AUROC drop or FNR inflation in minority strata, indicating the TF-IDF + ethnicity model encodes systematic disparity (a known issue with MIMIC-III clinical notes).
possible_sources: Chen et al. 'Why Is My Classifier Discriminatory?' NeurIPS 2018
Hardt et al. 'Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning' NeurIPS 2016
Röösli et al. 'Bias at warp speed: how AI may contribute to disparities gap in COVID-19' JAMIA 2021
Seyyed-Kalantari et al. 'CheXclusion' Pacific Symposium 2021

probe_designs.content: Mortality probabilities must be calibrated for clinical use. Compute reliability diagrams, Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Maximum Calibration Error, and Brier score globally and per ethnicity group. Decompose Brier into reliability/resolution/uncertainty. Healthy: ECE < 0.05 across all groups, points lie near the diagonal. Unhealthy: systematic over- or under-confidence, especially when calibration is good for the majority group but poor for minorities, which signals fairness violation in the calibration sense.
possible_sources: Guo et al. 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' ICML 2017
Pleiss et al. 'On Fairness and Calibration' NeurIPS 2017
Naeini et al. 'Obtaining Well Calibrated Probabilities' AAAI 2015

probe_designs.probe_name: Class Imbalance and Per-Group Prevalence Audit
probe_designs.content: ICU mortality is heavily imbalanced (~10–13% positive in MIMIC). Verify global positive rate, per-ethnicity prevalence, and per-ethnicity sample size. Check whether minority groups (e.g., Native American, 'OTHER') have <100 samples, which inflates variance of fairness metrics. Healthy: training/val/test stratified by both label and ethnicity; minority cohorts large enough for reliable estimation. Unhealthy: severe stratum imbalance such that one group dominates the loss and others are statistically invisible.
possible_sources: Johnson et al. 'MIMIC-III, a freely accessible critical care database' Scientific Data 2016
Harutyunyan et al. 'Multitask learning and benchmarking with clinical time series' Scientific Data 2019
He & Garcia 'Learning from Imbalanced Data' IEEE TKDE 2009

probe_designs.probe_name: Train/Validation/Test Loss Curves and Overfitting Diagnosis
probe_designs.content: Plot train vs. validation loss/AUROC across epochs. With a 10k-dim TF-IDF input on a typical MIMIC mortality cohort (~20k patients), overfitting is a primary risk. Healthy: validation AUROC plateaus close to training; small generalization gap (<0.03 AUROC). Unhealthy: monotonically increasing train AUROC while validation declines, or large gap (>0.1) — indicates need for L1/L2 regularization, dropout, or early stopping.
possible_sources: Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville 'Deep Learning' Chapter 7
Prechelt 'Early Stopping — but when?' Neural Networks 1998
Caruana et al. 'Overfitting in Neural Nets' NIPS 2000

probe_designs.probe_name: Ethnicity-Feature Ablation and Counterfactual Flip Test
probe_designs.content: Because ethnicity is fed as an explicit 6-dim one-hot, probe its causal influence: (a) zero-out the ethnicity vector at inference and re-evaluate AUROC and per-patient prediction shift; (b) counterfactually swap the one-hot to each of the other 5 groups and measure |Δp(mortality)|. Healthy: small counterfactual shifts unless ethnicity carries genuine clinical signal correlated with care patterns. Unhealthy: large prediction flips (>0.1) caused solely by ethnicity, indicating the model uses demographic identity as a shortcut rather than clinical content.
possible_sources: Kusner et al. 'Counterfactual Fairness' NeurIPS 2017
Garg et al. 'Counterfactual Fairness in Text Classification through Robustness' AIES 2019
Pfohl et al. 'An empirical characterization of fair ML for clinical risk prediction' JBI 2021

probe_designs.probe_name: Top-TF-IDF Token Attribution and Clinical Plausibility
probe_designs.content: Extract the highest-weighted TF-IDF tokens contributing to positive (mortality) and negative predictions, globally and per ethnicity group via permutation importance or logistic-regression coefficients / integrated gradients. Healthy: top features are clinically plausible (e.g., 'pressors', 'comfort care', 'withdraw', 'GCS 3', 'asystole'). Unhealthy: top features are administrative artifacts, hospital-unit codes, name fragments, or stop-word residue — suggesting leakage or weak preprocessing.
possible_sources: Ribeiro et al. 'Why Should I Trust You? Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier' KDD 2016
Lundberg & Lee 'A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions (SHAP)' NeurIPS 2017
Sushil et al. 'Patient representation learning and interpretable evaluation using clinical notes' JBI 2018

probe_designs.content: Clinical notes can contain post-hoc outcome language ('expired', 'pronounced dead', 'comfort measures only', 'DNR/DNI', 'autopsy', discharge-summary timestamps). Probe whether such tokens appear in the TF-IDF vocabulary and dominate predictions. Filter notes to those written before a fixed cutoff (e.g., first 24/48h of ICU stay) and re-train; healthy AUROC should drop modestly. Unhealthy: AUROC collapses from ~0.90 to ~0.70, confirming the original model relied on leakage rather than early predictive signal.
possible_sources: Boag et al. 'What's in a Note? Unpacking Predictive Value in Clinical Note Representations' AMIA 2018
Lehman et al. 'Inferring Multimodal Latent Topics from Electronic Medical Records' AMIA 2018
Ghassemi et al. 'A review of challenges and opportunities in machine learning for health' AMIA CRI 2020

probe_designs.content: Evaluate worst-group performance using DRO-style metrics: report worst-group AUROC, accuracy, and loss across ethnicity strata. Additionally test temporal shift (train on earlier admissions, test on later) and care-unit shift (MICU vs SICU vs CCU if available). Healthy: worst-group AUROC within ~0.03 of average; small degradation under shift. Unhealthy: worst-group AUROC much lower than average — the empirical risk minimizer is masking poor minority-group behavior.
possible_sources: Sagawa et al. 'Distributionally Robust Neural Networks' ICLR 2020
Koh et al. 'WILDS: A Benchmark of in-the-Wild Distribution Shifts' ICML 2021
Subbaswamy & Saria 'From development to deployment: dataset shift, source selection, and model robustness' Biostatistics 2020

probe_designs.probe_name: Threshold Sensitivity and Net Clinical Benefit
probe_designs.content: A scalar mortality probability is only useful if a threshold yields good clinical decisions. Sweep thresholds and report PR curves, sensitivity at fixed specificity (e.g., Sens@Spec=0.95), and decision-curve analysis (net benefit vs. treat-all and treat-none). Per-ethnicity decision curves reveal whether a single global threshold yields equitable clinical utility. Healthy: net benefit positive across realistic threshold ranges and consistent across groups. Unhealthy: optimal threshold differs sharply across groups, requiring group-specific operating points.
possible_sources: Vickers & Elkin 'Decision curve analysis: a novel method for evaluating prediction models' Med Decis Making 2006
Saito & Rehmsmeier 'The Precision-Recall Plot Is More Informative than the ROC Plot' PLOS ONE 2015
Pfohl et al. 'Net benefit, calibration, threshold selection, and training objectives for algorithmic fairness in healthcare' FAccT 2022

probe_designs.content: Train a linear probe on the model's penultimate-layer activations to predict ethnicity from notes alone (i.e., excluding the explicit ethnicity input). High probe accuracy indicates that demographic information is implicitly encoded in TF-IDF-driven representations and may be exploited by the classifier even if the one-hot is removed. Healthy: probe accuracy near base rate / language-balanced after notes-only training. Unhealthy: probe AUROC ≫ chance, meaning ethnicity is recoverable from notes — confounding attempts to mitigate bias by simply dropping the demographic input.
possible_sources: Elazar & Goldberg 'Adversarial Removal of Demographic Attributes from Text Data' EMNLP 2018
Zhang et al. 'Hurtful Words: Quantifying Biases in Clinical Contextual Word Embeddings' CHIL 2020
Ravfogel et al. 'Null It Out: Guarding Protected Attributes by Iterative Nullspace Projection' ACL 2020
